choroidal_neovascularization: Distinguished by the presence of abnormal blood vessels beneath the retina, unlike other classes.
Choroidal neovascularization shows subretinal or intraretinal fluid, which is absent in normal and drusen scans.
This class is associated with pigment epithelial detachment and leaky vessels, unlike diabetic macular edema.
The hyperreflective lesions and disruption of retinal layers set it apart from drusen and normal scans.
Unlike diabetic macular edema, the fluid is due to neovascularization rather than diabetic changes.
diabetic_macular_edema: Characterized by retinal thickening and cystoid spaces in the macula, unlike the subretinal deposits of drusen.
Diabetic macular edema shows diffuse or focal swelling, which is not seen in normal or neovascular scans.
The presence of intraretinal fluid and cystoid changes distinguishes it from choroidal neovascularization and drusen.
Unlike choroidal neovascularization, there are no abnormal blood vessels beneath the retina.
This class is specific to diabetic retinopathy and is not associated with subretinal neovascularization.
drusen: Identified by yellowish subretinal deposits beneath the retinal pigment epithelium, unlike fluid-filled or neovascular lesions.
Drusen appear as dome-shaped elevations of the RPE, which are not present in normal or edematous scans.
This class lacks the intraretinal fluid of diabetic macular edema and the neovascular features of CNV.
Drusen are associated with early macular degeneration, not with diabetic or neovascular changes.
The absence of significant fluid or abnormal vessels distinguishes drusen from other pathological classes.
normal_OCT_scan: Shows well-defined retinal layers with no evidence of pathology, unlike all other classes.
Normal OCT scans lack fluid, abnormal deposits, or neovascularization seen in disease states.
There are no signs of retinal thickening, subretinal deposits, or pigment epithelial detachment.
This class serves as a baseline for comparison with pathological OCT findings.
The intact retinal structure and regular reflectivity patterns distinguish normal scans from all abnormal classes.
